icd_category: J45.41 — Moderate persistent asthma with acute exacerbation
one_liner: A 22-year-old woman with a history of childhood asthma presents with acute-onset wheezing and shortness of breath after exposure to a cat.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient reports progressive dyspnea, chest tightness, and audible wheezing that began approximately 3 hours ago while visiting a friend who owns a cat. She has used her albuterol rescue inhaler 4 times in the past 2 hours without significant relief. She denies fever, productive cough, or recent URI symptoms. She ran out of her fluticasone inhaler 2 weeks ago and has not refilled it.
stage2.pmh: Asthma diagnosed at age 8, allergic rhinitis, eczema. Last ED visit for asthma exacerbation 1 year ago.
stage2.medications: Albuterol MDI PRN (currently using frequently), fluticasone MDI (non-compliant — ran out 2 weeks ago), cetirizine 10 mg daily.
stage2.allergies: No known drug allergies. Environmental: cats, dust mites, pollen.
stage2.family_history: Mother with asthma and allergic rhinitis. Father with eczema.
stage2.social_history: College student. Non-smoker. No alcohol or recreational drugs. Lives in a dormitory. No occupational exposures.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Young woman in moderate respiratory distress, speaking in short sentences, using accessory muscles. HEENT: Nasal mucosa edematous and pale. Lungs: Diffuse bilateral expiratory wheezing with prolonged expiratory phase; decreased air entry at bases. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Extremities: No cyanosis or edema. Skin: Mild eczematous patches on antecubital fossae.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Mild respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation; not yet in respiratory failure
Hypocapnia consistent with hyperventilation; a normalizing or rising pCO2 would be an ominous sign
Mild hypoxemia consistent with V/Q mismatch from bronchoconstriction
Normal; no evidence of infection driving the exacerbation
Mild peripheral eosinophilia consistent with allergic/atopic asthma phenotype
Elevated total IgE supports atopic/allergic etiology
Normal; rules out CHF as cause of dyspnea
Not significantly elevated; not suggestive of pneumonia or systemic infection
Normal; monitor with repeated albuterol use as beta-agonists can cause hypokalemia
Severely reduced PEF consistent with moderate-to-severe exacerbation

imaging_results.result: Hyperinflated lungs bilaterally. No infiltrates, effusions, or pneumothorax. No cardiomegaly.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Hyperinflation consistent with air trapping from bronchoconstriction; absence of consolidation argues against pneumonia

key_tests: Peak expiratory flow (PEF) or FEV1 measurement

stage5.final_clue: After administration of nebulized albuterol, ipratropium, and IV methylprednisolone, the patient's PEF improves to 310 L/min (68% predicted) and her SpO2 rises to 96% on room air, confirming a reversible obstructive process consistent with asthma exacerbation.

debrief.classic_presentation: Young atopic patient with known asthma presents with acute wheezing, dyspnea, and chest tightness after allergen exposure, with diffuse expiratory wheezing on exam and reduced PEF that improves with bronchodilators.
debrief.pathophysiology: Asthma exacerbations involve acute bronchospasm, airway inflammation, and mucus plugging triggered by allergens, infections, or irritants. In allergic asthma, inhaled allergens cross-link IgE on mast cells, triggering histamine and leukotriene release, causing smooth muscle contraction and mucosal edema. Non-compliance with inhaled corticosteroids allows chronic eosinophilic airway inflammation to persist, lowering the threshold for exacerbation.
debrief.treatment_overview: Acute management: supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 >92%, continuous or frequent nebulized albuterol +/- ipratropium, systemic corticosteroids (IV methylprednisolone or oral prednisone). Consider IV magnesium sulfate for severe exacerbations. Reassess PEF/FEV1 serially. Discharge with a prednisone taper (5 days), restart ICS/LABA controller therapy, asthma action plan, and allergen avoidance counseling.
high_yield_pearls: A normalizing or rising pCO2 on ABG during an asthma exacerbation is an ominous sign suggesting impending respiratory failure — the patient is tiring out.
PEF <25% predicted or inability to perform PEF indicates a severe/life-threatening exacerbation requiring ICU consideration.
Non-compliance with inhaled corticosteroids is the most common modifiable risk factor for asthma exacerbations.
The absence of wheezing in a severe exacerbation ('silent chest') indicates critically reduced airflow and is a pre-arrest sign.
Allergic asthma is part of the atopic triad: asthma, allergic rhinitis, and eczema — ask about all three.
sources: GINA 2024 Global Strategy for Asthma Management and Prevention
NAEPP Expert Panel Report 3 (EPR-3) Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Asthma
UpToDate: Acute exacerbations of asthma in adults
